title: DOACs — Overview
oneLiner: Direct oral anticoagulants for stroke prevention in AF and VTE treatment/prevention; key concerns are bleeding risk and correct dosing by renal function.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Major bleeding (intracranial, GI, retroperitoneal):** single finding = acute neurological deficit / sudden severe headache / hypotension with melaena or haematemesis
- **Thrombotic complications from non-adherence or missed doses:** single finding = sudden onset unilateral leg swelling (DVT) or pleuritic chest pain + dyspnoea (PE)
sections.Presentation:
- **Bleeding:** easy bruising, epistaxis, gingival bleeding, haematuria, melaena, haematemesis, haemoptysis, intracranial haemorrhage (headache, vomiting, focal neuro signs, decreased consciousness)
- **Thrombotic:** DVT (unilateral limb swelling, warmth, erythema), PE (acute dyspnoea, pleuritic pain, tachypnoea, haemoptysis), stroke/embolism (focal weakness, speech disturbance)
- **Routine review / initiation:** patient on or due to start DOAC; check adherence, side effects, renal function, weight, concomitant drugs
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely:** Bleeding related to DOAC vs. spontaneous bleeding from other cause (e.g. peptic ulcer, AV malformation) – history of DOAC use + abnormal coagulation profile points to DOAC
- **Thrombosis vs. other causes of limb swelling / dyspnoea** – DVT confirmed by USS; PE by CTPA or V/Q
- **Splitter:** DOACs do NOT require routine INR monitoring (unlike warfarin); if INR is elevated, suspect factor Xa inhibitor effect or liver disease – but not reliable for dose adjustment. Check anti-Xa activity if needed.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Vital signs (HR, BP, O2 sats, NEWS2), ECG for AF, urine dip for haematuria
- **Bloods:** FBC, coagulation screen (PT, aPTT – may be prolonged especially with dabigatran and rivaroxaban), renal function (urea, creatinine, eGFR, CrCl), LFTs, calcium, magnesium
- **Imaging:**
  - **Suspected GI bleed:** CT angiography or OGD/colonoscopy
  - **Suspected ICH:** CT head (non-contrast) as first line
  - **Suspected retroperitoneal bleed:** CT abdomen with contrast
  - **Suspected PE:** CTPA (or V/Q if contraindication)
  - **Suspected DVT:** venous duplex ultrasound
- **Special tests:** DOAC-specific anti-Xa or thrombin time if need to quantify effect (e.g. before emergency surgery)
- **Never-miss test:** **Renal function (eGFR/CrCl)** before prescribing or dosing – all DOACs require dose adjustment or avoidance in severe renal impairment
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Major bleeding:** Stop DOAC immediately. Give activated charcoal (50g) if last dose within 2 hours. Resuscitate with IV fluids, blood products. Consider reversal: idarucizumab (5g IV) for dabigatran; andexanet alfa for factor Xa inhibitors (dose per local protocol). Call haematology / senior.
  - **Thrombosis on DOAC:** Assess adherence. Check anti-Xa / drug levels. Consider switching to LMWH or alternative anticoagulant. Seek haematology advice.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Bleeding:** Identify and treat source. Consider tranexamic acid (not for ICH if thrombotic risk). Once stable, decide if/when to restart anticoagulation (e.g. AF patients with GI bleed: restart after haemostasis in 1-2 weeks usually, but discuss with senior).
  - **Routine initiation:** Check renal, weight, age, concurrent medications. Choose appropriate DOAC and dose based on indication (AF, VTE treatment, VTE prophylaxis). Provide anticoagulation card and counselling.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Annual renal function monitoring (more frequent if CKD). Address modifiable bleeding risk factors (e.g. BP control, NSAIDs, alcohol intake). Review at least yearly for ongoing need.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV fluids if bleeding/hypotension – 0.9% NaCl or balanced crystalloid at 500mL-1L stat then maintenance.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** If patient on therapeutic DOAC, no additional prophylaxis needed. If anticoagulation interrupted (e.g. for procedure), consider LMWH bridging per local policy for high-risk patients.
  - **Diet/NBM:** Normally no restriction unless planned procedure or GI bleed requiring nothing by mouth.
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 per protocol (minimum 4-hourly), plus neurological obs if ICH suspected.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (doses for standard indications – always consult BNF or local policy):**
  - **Apixaban:** AF / VTE treatment: 5mg PO BD (reduce to 2.5mg BD if any two of: age ≥80, weight ≤60kg, Cr ≥133μmol/L). VTE prophylaxis: 2.5mg PO BD.
  - **Dabigatran:** AF: 150mg PO BD (reduce to 110mg BD if age ≥80, or age 75-79 + one risk factor, or CrCl 30-50). VTE treatment: initially LMWH 5-10 days then dabigatran 150mg BD.
  - **Edoxaban:** AF / VTE treatment: 60mg PO OD (reduce to 30mg if CrCl 15-50, or weight ≤60kg).
  - **Rivaroxaban:** VTE prophylaxis: 10mg PO OD. AF / VTE treatment: 20mg PO OD (reduce to 15mg if CrCl 15-49).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Mechanical heart valves (all DOACs contraindicated)
  - Moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis
  - Antiphospholipid syndrome (triple positive) – use warfarin instead
  - Strong P-gp or CYP3A4 inducers (rifampicin, carbamazepine, phenytoin) reduce DOAC levels; strong inhibitors increase levels (azole antifungals, HIV protease inhibitors, amiodarone, verapamil)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - CrCl <15 mL/min: most DOACs contraindicated (dabigatran contraindicated if CrCl <30). Edoxaban not recommended if CrCl <15.
  - Hepatic impairment: avoid DOACs in Child-Pugh C; rivaroxaban contraindicated in Child-Pugh B or C.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing full-dose DOAC without dose reduction in patients meeting renal/age/weight criteria** – leads to accumulation and severe bleeding, especially with dabigatran (renally cleared). **Another critical error:** Using a DOAC in mechanical heart valve (contraindicated – increases valve thrombosis than warfarin).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Major bleeding (GI, ICH, retroperitoneal)
  - Acute kidney injury due to renal haematoma or hypovolaemia
  - Drug-drug interactions leading to supra/sub-therapeutic levels
- **Chronic:**
  - Progressive renal impairment (especially with dabigatran)
  - Osteoporosis (possible, less data; but consider calcium/vitamin D)
  - Bleeding with cumulative comorbidities (diverticulosis, peptic ulcer)
sections.Prognosis: - DOACs reduce stroke risk by ~60-70% in AF vs placebo, and by ~20% vs warfarin with lower ICH rates. Major bleeding event mortality ~10-20%. Adherence is crucial – missed doses increase stroke/embolism risk.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always document the DOAC indication, dose, and next review date** in the drug chart and discharge summary.
- **Before starting, calculate stroke risk (CHA₂DS₂-VASc) and bleeding risk (HAS-BLED)** – use HAS-BLED to address modifiable risks, not to withhold DOAC.
- **Check renal function at baseline and at least annually** – more frequently if eGFR <60 or >75 years.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not routinely monitor PT/aPTT or INR** – they are unreliable for dose adjustment. Anti-Xa assays are specific but not for routine monitoring.
- **Do not start a DOAC in acute stroke without confirming no haemorrhage** (CT). Wait 24-48h (longer if large infarct) before initiating anticoagulation.
- **Do not prescribe a DOAC for VTE in patients with antiphospholipid syndrome** (especially triple positive) – use warfarin with target INR 2-3.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Severe headache/vision changes, blood in urine/stool, black tarry stools, coughing blood, heavy bruising without injury, or symptoms of DVT/PE (leg swelling, dyspnoea).
- **Follow-up:** GP review within 2 weeks for initial check; anticoagulation clinic or pharmacy review within 1 month for adherence and side effects. Repeat renal function in 3 months for first year.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / haematology / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5, or lactate >2 mmol/L, or GCS drop of ≥2
  - Suspected massive bleeding (hypotension, tachycardia, ?haemorrhagic shock)
  - Suspected ICH on CT or clinical signs
  - Need for reversal agent (idarucizumab/andexanet) – discuss with haematology first
  - Anaphylaxis to reversal agent (rare)
  - Acute thrombosis despite adequate DOAC therapy (consider non-adherence, antiphospholipid syndrome, or need for change to warfarin/LMWH)

rawMarkdown:
## DOACs — Overview
**One-liner:** Direct oral anticoagulants for stroke prevention in AF and VTE treatment/prevention; key concerns are bleeding risk and correct dosing by renal function.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Major bleeding (intracranial, GI, retroperitoneal):** single finding = acute neurological deficit / sudden severe headache / hypotension with melaena or haematemesis
- **Thrombotic complications from non-adherence or missed doses:** single finding = sudden onset unilateral leg swelling (DVT) or pleuritic chest pain + dyspnoea (PE)

### Presentation
- **Bleeding:** easy bruising, epistaxis, gingival bleeding, haematuria, melaena, haematemesis, haemoptysis, intracranial haemorrhage (headache, vomiting, focal neuro signs, decreased consciousness)
- **Thrombotic:** DVT (unilateral limb swelling, warmth, erythema), PE (acute dyspnoea, pleuritic pain, tachypnoea, haemoptysis), stroke/embolism (focal weakness, speech disturbance)
- **Routine review / initiation:** patient on or due to start DOAC; check adherence, side effects, renal function, weight, concomitant drugs

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely:** Bleeding related to DOAC vs. spontaneous bleeding from other cause (e.g. peptic ulcer, AV malformation) – history of DOAC use + abnormal coagulation profile points to DOAC
- **Thrombosis vs. other causes of limb swelling / dyspnoea** – DVT confirmed by USS; PE by CTPA or V/Q
- **Splitter:** DOACs do NOT require routine INR monitoring (unlike warfarin); if INR is elevated, suspect factor Xa inhibitor effect or liver disease – but not reliable for dose adjustment. Check anti-Xa activity if needed.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Vital signs (HR, BP, O2 sats, NEWS2), ECG for AF, urine dip for haematuria
- **Bloods:** FBC, coagulation screen (PT, aPTT – may be prolonged especially with dabigatran and rivaroxaban), renal function (urea, creatinine, eGFR, CrCl), LFTs, calcium, magnesium
- **Imaging:**
  - **Suspected GI bleed:** CT angiography or OGD/colonoscopy
  - **Suspected ICH:** CT head (non-contrast) as first line
  - **Suspected retroperitoneal bleed:** CT abdomen with contrast
  - **Suspected PE:** CTPA (or V/Q if contraindication)
  - **Suspected DVT:** venous duplex ultrasound
- **Special tests:** DOAC-specific anti-Xa or thrombin time if need to quantify effect (e.g. before emergency surgery)
- **Never-miss test:** **Renal function (eGFR/CrCl)** before prescribing or dosing – all DOACs require dose adjustment or avoidance in severe renal impairment

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Major bleeding:** Stop DOAC immediately. Give activated charcoal (50g) if last dose within 2 hours. Resuscitate with IV fluids, blood products. Consider reversal: idarucizumab (5g IV) for dabigatran; andexanet alfa for factor Xa inhibitors (dose per local protocol). Call haematology / senior.
  - **Thrombosis on DOAC:** Assess adherence. Check anti-Xa / drug levels. Consider switching to LMWH or alternative anticoagulant. Seek haematology advice.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Bleeding:** Identify and treat source. Consider tranexamic acid (not for ICH if thrombotic risk). Once stable, decide if/when to restart anticoagulation (e.g. AF patients with GI bleed: restart after haemostasis in 1-2 weeks usually, but discuss with senior).
  - **Routine initiation:** Check renal, weight, age, concurrent medications. Choose appropriate DOAC and dose based on indication (AF, VTE treatment, VTE prophylaxis). Provide anticoagulation card and counselling.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Annual renal function monitoring (more frequent if CKD). Address modifiable bleeding risk factors (e.g. BP control, NSAIDs, alcohol intake). Review at least yearly for ongoing need.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV fluids if bleeding/hypotension – 0.9% NaCl or balanced crystalloid at 500mL-1L stat then maintenance.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** If patient on therapeutic DOAC, no additional prophylaxis needed. If anticoagulation interrupted (e.g. for procedure), consider LMWH bridging per local policy for high-risk patients.
  - **Diet/NBM:** Normally no restriction unless planned procedure or GI bleed requiring nothing by mouth.
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 per protocol (minimum 4-hourly), plus neurological obs if ICH suspected.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (doses for standard indications – always consult BNF or local policy):**
  - **Apixaban:** AF / VTE treatment: 5mg PO BD (reduce to 2.5mg BD if any two of: age ≥80, weight ≤60kg, Cr ≥133μmol/L). VTE prophylaxis: 2.5mg PO BD.
  - **Dabigatran:** AF: 150mg PO BD (reduce to 110mg BD if age ≥80, or age 75-79 + one risk factor, or CrCl 30-50). VTE treatment: initially LMWH 5-10 days then dabigatran 150mg BD.
  - **Edoxaban:** AF / VTE treatment: 60mg PO OD (reduce to 30mg if CrCl 15-50, or weight ≤60kg).
  - **Rivaroxaban:** VTE prophylaxis: 10mg PO OD. AF / VTE treatment: 20mg PO OD (reduce to 15mg if CrCl 15-49).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Mechanical heart valves (all DOACs contraindicated)
  - Moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis
  - Antiphospholipid syndrome (triple positive) – use warfarin instead
  - Strong P-gp or CYP3A4 inducers (rifampicin, carbamazepine, phenytoin) reduce DOAC levels; strong inhibitors increase levels (azole antifungals, HIV protease inhibitors, amiodarone, verapamil)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - CrCl <15 mL/min: most DOACs contraindicated (dabigatran contraindicated if CrCl <30). Edoxaban not recommended if CrCl <15.
  - Hepatic impairment: avoid DOACs in Child-Pugh C; rivaroxaban contraindicated in Child-Pugh B or C.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing full-dose DOAC without dose reduction in patients meeting renal/age/weight criteria** – leads to accumulation and severe bleeding, especially with dabigatran (renally cleared). **Another critical error:** Using a DOAC in mechanical heart valve (contraindicated – increases valve thrombosis than warfarin).

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Major bleeding (GI, ICH, retroperitoneal)
  - Acute kidney injury due to renal haematoma or hypovolaemia
  - Drug-drug interactions leading to supra/sub-therapeutic levels
- **Chronic:**
  - Progressive renal impairment (especially with dabigatran)
  - Osteoporosis (possible, less data; but consider calcium/vitamin D)
  - Bleeding with cumulative comorbidities (diverticulosis, peptic ulcer)

### Prognosis
- DOACs reduce stroke risk by ~60-70% in AF vs placebo, and by ~20% vs warfarin with lower ICH rates. Major bleeding event mortality ~10-20%. Adherence is crucial – missed doses increase stroke/embolism risk.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always document the DOAC indication, dose, and next review date** in the drug chart and discharge summary.
- **Before starting, calculate stroke risk (CHA₂DS₂-VASc) and bleeding risk (HAS-BLED)** – use HAS-BLED to address modifiable risks, not to withhold DOAC.
- **Check renal function at baseline and at least annually** – more frequently if eGFR <60 or >75 years.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not routinely monitor PT/aPTT or INR** – they are unreliable for dose adjustment. Anti-Xa assays are specific but not for routine monitoring.
- **Do not start a DOAC in acute stroke without confirming no haemorrhage** (CT). Wait 24-48h (longer if large infarct) before initiating anticoagulation.
- **Do not prescribe a DOAC for VTE in patients with antiphospholipid syndrome** (especially triple positive) – use warfarin with target INR 2-3.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Severe headache/vision changes, blood in urine/stool, black tarry stools, coughing blood, heavy bruising without injury, or symptoms of DVT/PE (leg swelling, dyspnoea).
- **Follow-up:** GP review within 2 weeks for initial check; anticoagulation clinic or pharmacy review within 1 month for adherence and side effects. Repeat renal function in 3 months for first year.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / haematology / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5, or lactate >2 mmol/L, or GCS drop of ≥2
  - Suspected massive bleeding (hypotension, tachycardia, ?haemorrhagic shock)
  - Suspected ICH on CT or clinical signs
  - Need for reversal agent (idarucizumab/andexanet) – discuss with haematology first
  - Anaphylaxis to reversal agent (rare)
  - Acute thrombosis despite adequate DOAC therapy (consider non-adherence, antiphospholipid syndrome, or need for change to warfarin/LMWH)
